advaita.rendering: From the beginning of creation I declared two-fold adherence (niṣṭhā) in this world for those qualified by the three castes: the path of knowledge (jñāna-yoga) for the Sāṅkhyas — those who have discriminated Self from non-Self, renounced from brahmacarya itself, and stand established in Brahman alone — and the path of action (karma-yoga) for the yogins still engaged in rites. These two niṣṭhās are not concurrent options for one person; their simultaneous practice is impossible, because they are mutually contradictory (paraspara-virodha). Karma-yoga leads to knowledge-niṣṭhā as its instrumental cause; jñāna-niṣṭhā alone, won through that instrumentality, is the independent means to liberation.
advaita.key_doctrinal_move: Sequential and hierarchical: karma-yoga is subsidiary preparation (upāya), jñāna-niṣṭhā is the sole liberating end. No samuccaya (conjunction) of the two is admitted.
advaita.bhashya_anchor: Śaṅkara explicitly: 'karmaniṣṭhāyā jñānanisṭhāprāptiheuttvena puruṣārthaheuttvam na svātantryeṇa' — karma-niṣṭhā derives its value only as means to jñāna-niṣṭhā, never independently.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: The Lord declared two coordinate (asaṃkīrṇa) modes of adherence appropriate to the two grades of qualified seekers (adhikārin) that populate this variegated world: jñāna-yoga for the Sāṅkhyas whose minds are already undisturbed, and karma-yoga for the yogins whose intellects are still agitated by sense-objects. Not everyone who has kindled the desire for liberation immediately qualifies for jñāna-yoga; one must first, through desireless action performed as pure worship of the Supreme Person (paramapuruṣārādhana), dissolve the impurities of the mind and render the senses calm. The two niṣṭhās are thus coordinate stages of a single upward arc, neither superior in itself, but each sovereign within its adhikāra.
viśiṣṭādvaita.key_doctrinal_move: Coordinate and adhikāra-relative: both niṣṭhās are legitimate paths; karma-yoga is not merely preparatory but is itself bhagavad-ārādhana (worship of God) and therefore intrinsically valuable.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Rāmānuja: 'asaṃkīrṇa eva mayā uktā' — declared unmixed, each for its own adhikārin. And 18.46 cited: 'svakarmṇā tam abhyarcya siddhiṃ vindati mānavaḥ' — action itself is worship leading to perfection.

dvaita.rendering: Even though knowledge (buddhi) is superior in itself, the Lord here declares that two kinds of persons exist in the world and each has his divinely assigned station: those like Sanaka who, having renounced action by divine will (īśvara-icchayā), abide in jñāna-niṣṭhā alone, and those like Janaka who remain in action yet possess jñāna simultaneously. Arjuna belongs to the Janaka-type — fit for knowledge precisely through action, not through its abandonment. Even those in jñāna-niṣṭhā act when Hari wills it, as the example of Priyavrata shows; the division is therefore not between knowledge and ignorance but between two forms of obedience to the Lord's will.
dvaita.key_doctrinal_move: Both niṣṭhās are expressions of divine will (īśvara-icchā); the jīva's adhikāra is assigned by Hari, not self-selected. Janaka-archetype validates karma for qualified jñānins.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: Madhva: 'īśvara-icchayā viniveśita-karmādhikāraḥ' (Bhāg. 5.1.23 cited) — fitness for action is itself installed by the Lord's will.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: The Lord corrects Arjuna's confusion: the two-fold niṣṭhā he declared earlier is not a rivalry between equal independent paths to liberation, but a gracious distribution according to adhikāra — for the Sāṅkhyas who have renounced everything, jñāna-yoga as complete relinquishment; for those qualified in the karma-yoga described, action in the ordained form. Each seeker is fit only for what is consonant with his own capacity (svādhikārānusāra), so no anxiety about which path to choose is appropriate. The three modes — yoga, jñāna, and bhakti — are each independently capable of yielding the supreme end, and the Lord graciously provides all three.
śuddhādvaita.key_doctrinal_move: Adhikāra-determined plurality: yoga, jñāna, and bhakti are coordinate self-sufficient paths; the Lord's grace (prasāda) distributes seekers to their appropriate gate. No single path is subordinated.
śuddhādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Vallabha: 'svādhikārānusāreṇaiva sarvaṃ yogyam' — all is fitting only when aligned with one's own adhikāra. And: 'yoga-jñānaṃ ca bhaktiś ca' — all three cited as independent mokṣa-hetus.

bhakti.rendering: Śrīdhara clarifies that the Lord never declared two entirely independent (paraspara-nirapekṣa) means of liberation; rather, He declared one single Brahman-niṣṭhā (eka brahma-niṣṭhā) expressed in two modes according to the purity or impurity of the inner instrument (antaḥkaraṇa). For those of purified mind already standing on the jñāna-bhūmikā, the mode is jñāna-yoga — remaining absorbed in contemplation. For those still ascending toward that platform through purification, the mode is karma-yoga as the means of antaḥkaraṇa-śuddhi. The verse 2.39 — 'eṣā te abhihitā sāṅkhye buddhir yoge tv imāṃ śṛṇu' — is Śrīdhara's own proof that both modes were announced as aspects of one niṣṭhā, not two.
bhakti.key_doctrinal_move: One niṣṭhā in two modes: the distinction is purely a function of antaḥkaraṇa-śuddhi (purity of inner instrument), not ontological difference in path. Karma-yoga purifies; jñāna-yoga crowns.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Śrīdhara: 'dvābhyām ekā brahma-niṣṭhoktā... guṇapradhānabhūtayos tayoḥ svātantryānupapatteḥ' — the two aspects cannot stand independently because one is subordinate (guṇa) to the other (pradhāna).

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Madhusūdana confirms that the singular form 'niṣṭhā' is deliberate — there is one niṣṭhā that appears two-fold only by the distinction of stages (bhūmikā-bheda), not two independently valid alternatives. For those of purified antaḥkaraṇa who have reached the jñāna-bhūmikā through renunciation from brahmacarya itself, jñāna-yoga is their form of that one niṣṭhā. For those of impure antaḥkaraṇa not yet ascended to that platform, karma-yoga is their form — its purpose being antaḥkaraṇa-śuddhi, which then opens access to jñāna-niṣṭhā. Neither samuccaya (combination) nor vikalpa (option) between the two is admissible; the sequence is strict. Kṛṣṇa addresses Arjuna as 'anagha' (sinless) to signal his existing fitness for instruction, and to indicate that the Lord's purpose is pure benefit, not confusion.
advaita-bhakti.key_doctrinal_move: Śaṅkara's sequential reading is preserved but softened by the explicit single-niṣṭhā grammar; the verse's singular 'niṣṭhā' is itself Madhusūdana's grammatical proof against dual-path readings.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Madhusūdana: 'ekā niṣṭhā sādhyasādhanāvasthābhedena dviprakārā na tu dve eva svatantre niṣṭhe iti kaṭhayituṃ niṣṭhety ekavacanaṃ' — the grammatical singular is the argument.

so_what_questions: If karma-yoga and jñāna-yoga are sequential stages of a single path (Śaṅkara, Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana), what observable signal tells a practitioner they have completed the first stage and may enter the second — and who adjudicates that transition?

Rāmānuja and Madhva treat both niṣṭhās as coordinate options assigned by adhikāra; how does one determine one's own adhikāra without circular self-assessment, and what prevents the convenient self-assignment to whichever path demands less?

The verse's grammatical singular 'niṣṭhā' (one adherence) is load-bearing for Madhusūdana's entire argument. Does the philological case hold against the schools that read two independent paths, and what does the answer imply for how bhāṣya-reading itself works?

Madhva introduces divine will (īśvara-icchā) as the ultimate allocator of adhikāra. Does this dissolve human agency in path-selection, or redefine it as discernment of one's assigned station — and how does this interact with the Gītā's repeated imperatives to Arjuna?

Vallabha lists yoga, jñāna, and bhakti as three coordinate self-sufficient paths. If all three reach the same end, what principle prevents further proliferation — is every sincere practice a legitimate niṣṭhā, or is the three-fold list a closed canon?

All schools agree that 'Sāṅkhyas' here does not mean the classical Sāṅkhya school of Kapila but denotes a class of practitioners. What determines who counts as a 'Sāṅkhya' in this context — ontological position, psychological state, or institutional status (renunciate vs householder)?

Kṛṣṇa says 'purā proktā mayā' — declared by me of old. All schools must explain what 'old' means (Vedic creation? prior chapters? prior cosmic cycle). Which school's answer is most coherent with the verse's insistence on temporal priority, and what turns on getting this right?

advaita.application: A professional who finds their work spiritually hollow should not immediately abandon it for a monastery. Śaṅkara's reading prescribes a preparatory phase: perform every task without personal stake in outcome (niṣkāma), treating it as antaḥkaraṇa-śuddhi in motion. Renunciation is earned, not grabbed — only when the inner instrument is genuinely purified does one qualify for the contemplative life. Premature renunciation is another form of evasion.
advaita.concrete_scenario: A software engineer feeling called to full-time study of Vedānta should first ask: Is my current work being done with full detachment? If clients' approval still produces elation and rejection still produces depression, the karma-bhūmikā is not yet complete.

viśiṣṭādvaita.application: Rāmānuja's coordinate reading means that householder life — work, family, civic duty — is not spiritually inferior to monastic life; it is its own complete form of worship when performed as paramapuruṣārādhana. Every task re-framed as an offering to Bhagavān transforms karma-yoga from moral hygiene into devotional practice. There is no obligation to graduate from it.
viśiṣṭādvaita.concrete_scenario: A parent managing the logistics of a household — groceries, school runs, bill payments — can treat each act as an offering to the Lord who pervades all beings. The act is not preparation for something more sacred; it IS the sacred practice for one of undisturbed but not yet inward-turned mind.

dvaita.application: Madhva's Janaka-archetype validates engaged leadership: a CEO, a doctor, a judge who acts with complete competence and knowledge yet remains embedded in action is not spiritually compromised. Their adhikāra was assigned by the Lord; to abandon it in search of the Sanaka-path without divine sanction would itself be a transgression. Discernment here means recognising which archetype you have been placed into.
dvaita.concrete_scenario: A surgeon who has attained considerable equanimity but whose skills are still needed in the world should not conclude that the Sanaka-path (complete withdrawal) is now available to them. Madhva asks: has the Lord released you from this post? If patients keep appearing, the answer is not yet.

śuddhādvaita.application: Vallabha's grace-distribution reading removes the anxious self-audit that Śaṅkara's sequential model can generate. The practitioner need not constantly measure whether they have completed karma-yoga and may now 'advance' to jñāna-yoga. Kṛṣṇa's prasāda places each person on the appropriate path; the task is wholehearted engagement with the path one finds oneself on, not restless migration between them.
śuddhādvaita.concrete_scenario: A devotee drawn naturally to singing kirtan, preparing prasādam, and temple service should not feel guilty that they are 'not doing enough jñāna.' Vallabha's reading says: bhakti is itself a sovereign mokṣa-hetu; the impulse toward it is itself the Lord's grace allocating you to your gate.

bhakti.application: Śrīdhara's one-niṣṭhā-two-modes reading cures a common confusion: people who have begun contemplative practice and find it interrupted by life-duties interpret the interruption as spiritual failure. Śrīdhara says the duties ARE the practice at that stage — antaḥkaraṇa-śuddhi is the work, not a detour from the work. The sign of completion is not a dramatic breakthrough but a steady, natural disinclination toward sense-objects.
bhakti.concrete_scenario: A meditator who cannot yet sit for long periods without distraction should stop lamenting the gap between their sitting-practice and their goal. The gap IS the curriculum — each moment of patient return to duty, without grasping or aversion, is the karma-yoga mode of the one brahma-niṣṭhā.

advaita-bhakti.application: Madhusūdana's grammatical argument — one niṣṭhā, two stages — carries a practical corollary: the practitioner should hold both stages in a single vision rather than treating them as alien regimes. Karma-yoga is not abandoned at the jñāna stage; its form changes. The executive who reaches equanimity does not stop making decisions — they make them from a different ground. The grammar of singular niṣṭhā is the grammar of continuous practice.
advaita-bhakti.concrete_scenario: A teacher who has spent years in classroom service and finds Vedānta study opening naturally should not experience this as a career change but as the same niṣṭhā entering its second mode. The students are still the field; the relationship to outcome has shifted. Madhusūdana would say: you were always on the one path; the platform changed underfoot.

self_assessment: All six bhāṣyas present with substantive Sanskrit prose; renderings anchored directly in cited bhāṣya arguments; Śrīdhara payload was clean Sanskrit with no HTML artifacts; the advaita-bhakti (Madhusūdana) and bhakti (Śrīdhara) schools converge on the grammatical singular 'niṣṭhā' as proof of one-path-two-modes, creating a genuine doctrinal fault-line against Vallabha's coordinate-three-paths reading and Madhva's divine-will-assignment reading — that divergence is real and is surfaced in both renderings and so-what questions.
